title: Czersk, Masovian Voivodeship

infobox.value: * [Aleksandrów](/wiki/Aleksandr%C3%B3w,_Piaseczno_County "Aleksandrów, Piaseczno County") * [Baniocha](/wiki/Baniocha "Baniocha") * [Borki](/wiki/Borki,_Piaseczno_County "Borki, Piaseczno County") * [Brześce](/wiki/Brze%C5%9Bce,_Masovian_Voivodeship "Brześce, Masovian Voivodeship") * [Brzumin](/wiki/Brzumin "Brzumin") * [Buczynów](/wiki/Buczyn%C3%B3w "Buczynów") * [Cendrowice](/wiki/Cendrowice "Cendrowice") * [Coniew](/wiki/Coniew "Coniew") * [Czachówek](/wiki/Czach%C3%B3wek,_Masovian_Voivodeship "Czachówek, Masovian Voivodeship") * [Czaplin](/wiki/Czaplin "Czaplin") * [Czaplinek](/wiki/Czaplinek,_Masovian_Voiv

(Polish pronunciation: [[t͡ʂɛrsk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-Czersk.ogg "File:Pl-Czersk.ogg")) is a [settlement](/wiki/Human_settlement "Human settlement") in the administrative district of [Gmina Góra Kalwaria](/wiki/Gmina_G%C3%B3ra_Kalwaria "Gmina Góra Kalwaria"), within [Piaseczno County](/wiki/Piaseczno_County "Piaseczno County"), [Masovian Voivodeship](/wiki/Masovian_Voivodeship "Masovian Voivodeship"), in east-central Poland.[[1]](#cite_note-TERYT-1) The village also lies on the [Czersk Lake](/w/index.php?title=Czersk_Lake&action=edit&redlink=1 "Czersk Lake (page does not exist)") (size: 9 [hectares](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare")), which is an [oxbow lake](/wiki/Oxbow_lake "Oxbow lake") of the [Vistula](/wiki/Vistula "Vistula").

**Czersk** (Polish pronunciation: [[t͡ʂɛrsk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-Czersk.ogg "File:Pl-Czersk.ogg")) is a [settlement](/wiki/Human_settlement "Human settlement") in the administrative district of [Gmina Góra Kalwaria](/wiki/Gmina_G%C3%B3ra_Kalwaria "Gmina Góra Kalwaria"), within [Piaseczno County](/wiki/Piaseczno_County "Piaseczno County"), [Masovian Voivodeship](/wiki/Masovian_Voivodeship "Masovian Voivodeship"), in east-central Poland.[[1]](#cite_note-TERYT-1) The village also lies on the [Czersk Lake](/w/index.php?title=Czersk_Lake&action=edit&redlink=1 "Czersk Lake (page does not exist)") (size: 9 [hectares](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare")), which is an [oxbow lake](/wiki/Oxbow_lake "Oxbow lake") of the [Vistula](/wiki/Vistula "Vistula").

Czersk was an important settlement in the past and is one of the oldest [Mazovian](/wiki/Mazovia "Mazovia") cities, famous for ruins of a medieval castle constructed in the late 14th century. It was a royal town of the [Crown of the Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland") in the past. In 1247–1526, it was the capital of the [Duchy of Czersk](/wiki/Duchy_of_Czersk "Duchy of Czersk"), one of medieval Polish duchies. After incorporation of Mazovia into Poland (1526), the duchy was turned into the [Czersk Land](/wiki/Czersk_Land "Czersk Land") (see [ziemia](/wiki/Ziemia "Ziemia")), part of [Masovian Voivodeship (1526–1795)](/wiki/Masovian_Voivodeship_(1526%E2%80%931795) "Masovian Voivodeship (1526–1795)"). The Land of Czersk was divided into four [counties](/wiki/Powiat "Powiat") - those of Czersk, [Grójec](/wiki/Gr%C3%B3jec "Grójec"), [Garwolin](/wiki/Garwolin "Garwolin") (since 1539), and [Warka](/wiki/Warka "Warka"). Czersk itself was the seat of [castellans](/wiki/Castellan "Castellan"), who also were [senators](/wiki/Senator "Senator") of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth").

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Czersk,_Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

Czersk was first mentioned in the [Laurentian Codex](/wiki/Laurentian_Codex "Laurentian Codex"), in which under the date 1142, it is spelled as Чьрньскъ (Čьrnьsk, Cz(i)rn(i)sk). In Polish-language sources, it was spelled Cirnsk (1236-1237), Cyrnsko (1242), Czyersko (1456), and later, Czyrnsk or Czyrnsko. These forms were later simplified into Czyrsko, which in late [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") changed into Czersk. The name probably comes from the Czarna river, a tributary to the Vistula River.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Czersk,_Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Czersk_%2841381547%29.jpg/250px-Czersk_%2841381547%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Czersk_(41381547).jpg)

[Czersk Castle](/wiki/Czersk_Castle "Czersk Castle") in the interbellum

First Slavic [gord](/wiki/Gord_(archaeology) "Gord (archaeology)") was established on a local hill in the 11th century, in a location of a 7th-century settlement, near a Vistula river [ford](/wiki/Ford_(crossing) "Ford (crossing)"). In the 12th century, Czersk became the seat of a [castellan](/wiki/Castellan "Castellan"), and in 1252, of a Roman Catholic [archdeacon](/wiki/Archdeacon "Archdeacon"), subjected to the Bishop of [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań"), which turned the town into an important political center of Mazovia. In the same year, Czersk was named a capital of a Mazovian duchy.

Officially, Czersk received town charter in 1350, but long before that time, it had an urban character, with a mid-13th century market square. In 1388, Duke of Mazovia [Janusz I of Warsaw](/wiki/Janusz_I_of_Warsaw "Janusz I of Warsaw") ordered construction of a Gothic castle with two towers and a tower gate. The castle was completed in 1410. Three years later, the capital of the duchy was moved to better located [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), and Czersk began a slow decline. Furthermore, in the 14th century the Vistula river bed moved eastwards, and the town found itself located a few kilometers away from the strategic river.

During the reign of King Czersk [Sigismund I the Old](/wiki/Sigismund_I_the_Old "Sigismund I the Old"), Czersk became one of favourite places of his wife, Queen [Bona Sforza](/wiki/Bona_Sforza "Bona Sforza"). She spent a lot of time here, and on her initiative, Czersk returned as one of main centers of the region. A brewery was opened, trade flourished, local produce was very popular across [Mazovia](/wiki/Mazovia "Mazovia"). The town prospered until the [Swedish invasion of Poland](/wiki/Deluge_(history) "Deluge (history)"), when, after the [Battle of Warka](/wiki/Battle_of_Warka "Battle of Warka") (1656), Czersk, together with the castle, was completely destroyed by the retreating Swedish units. The town never recovered from the destruction; in 1762-1766 Crown Marshall [Franciszek Bieliński](/wiki/Franciszek_Bieli%C5%84ski "Franciszek Bieliński") tried to rebuild the castle, but his project was not completed due to [Partitions of Poland](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland").

In 1815, Czersk became part of Russian-controlled [Congress Poland](/wiki/Congress_Poland "Congress Poland"), and in 1869, as a punishment for the [January Uprising](/wiki/January_Uprising "January Uprising"), Czersk was stripped of its town rights, becoming a village. Once one of the largest medieval towns of Poland, and second largest town of Mazovia (after [Płock](/wiki/P%C5%82ock "Płock")), Czersk now is a small village, with population less than 1,000. Czersk still has a medieval [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan"), with a large market square in the middle.

## Czersk Castle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Czersk,_Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Czersk Castle")]

Main article: [Czersk Castle](/wiki/Czersk_Castle "Czersk Castle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Zamek_ksi%C4%85%C5%BC%C4%85t_mazowieckich_w_Czersku_1.jpg/250px-Zamek_ksi%C4%85%C5%BC%C4%85t_mazowieckich_w_Czersku_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zamek_ksi%C4%85%C5%BC%C4%85t_mazowieckich_w_Czersku_1.jpg)

Image of Czersk Castle just before entrance bridge

In the 11th century, during the reign of either [Bolesław III Wrymouth](/wiki/Boles%C5%82aw_III_Wrymouth "Bolesław III Wrymouth") or [Władysław I Herman](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_I_Herman "Władysław I Herman"), a wooden [gord](/wiki/Gord_(archaeology) "Gord (archaeology)") existed in the location of the castle. The gord later became administrative center of the Duchy of Czersk, mentioned in 1224 in the documents of [Castellan](/wiki/Castellan "Castellan") of Czersk, Piotr Pilch. In 1229, Duke [Konrad I of Masovia](/wiki/Konrad_I_of_Masovia "Konrad I of Masovia") imprisoned here Duke [Henry I the Bearded](/wiki/Henry_I_the_Bearded "Henry I the Bearded"), who was kept in the castle's dungeon. In 1239, Konrad imprisoned here another Duke, [Bolesław V the Chaste](/wiki/Boles%C5%82aw_V_the_Chaste "Bolesław V the Chaste").

In 1350, the gord was partly destroyed in a Lithuanian raid, and in 1388, Duke of Mazovia [Janusz I of Warsaw](/wiki/Janusz_I_of_Warsaw "Janusz I of Warsaw") ordered construction of a Gothic castle with two towers and a tower gate. The castle became one of main residences of Janusz I; it was here that he died on December 8, 1429.

In 1526, Mazovia was incorporated into the [Crown of the Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland"), and the Czersk Castle became a royal property. In 1547, it was administered by Queen [Bona Sforza](/wiki/Bona_Sforza "Bona Sforza"), who in 1549 built here a residence, called *Wielki Dom*. The castle was almost completely destroyed in 1656, during the [Swedish invasion of Poland](/wiki/Deluge_(history) "Deluge (history)"). In 1762–1766, the [Starosta](/wiki/Starosta "Starosta") of Czersk and Crown Marshall tried to rebuild the complex, as he planned to place here archives and local court. His plans failed due to the [Partitions of Poland](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland"), and the castle now is a permanent ruin. It is open to visitors, who admire the preserved walls and three towers. Inside the complex, there are foundations of St. Peter church.
